.. tip::
    In addition to Swagger, Litestar serves the documentation from the generated
    OpenAPI schema with `ReDoc <https://redocly.com/>`_ and
    `Stoplight Elements <https://stoplight.io/open-source/elements/>`_. You can browse
    to http://127.0.0.1:8000/schema/redoc and http://127.0.0.1:8000/schema/elements to
    view each, respectively.

[2] (docs/PYPI_README.md § OpenAPI)
## OpenAPI
### OpenAPI

Litestar has custom logic to generate OpenAPI 3.1.0 schema, include optional generation of examples using the
[`polyfactory`](https://pypi.org/project/polyfactory/) library.

#### ReDoc, Swagger-UI and Stoplight Elements API Documentation

Litestar serves the documentation from the generated OpenAPI schema with:

- [ReDoc](https://redoc.ly/)
- [Swagger-UI](https://swagger.io/tools/swagger-ui/)
- [Stoplight Elements](https://github.com/stoplightio/elements)
- [RapiDoc](https://rapidocweb.com/)

All these are available and enabled by default.

[3] (docs/usage/openapi/schema_generation.rst)
app = Litestar(
       route_handlers=[public_path_handler, internal_path_handler],
       openapi_config=OpenAPIConfig(
           title="my api",
           version="1.0.0",
           tags=[
               Tag(name="public", description="This endpoint is for external users"),
               Tag(name="internal", description="This endpoint is for internal users"),
           ],
           security=[{"BearerToken": []}],
           components=Components(
               security_schemes={
                   "BearerToken": SecurityScheme(
                       type="http",
                       scheme="bearer",
                   )
               },
           ),
       ),
   )

Accessing the OpenAPI schema in code
------------------------------------

The OpenAPI schema is generated during the :class:`Litestar <litestar.app.Litestar>` app's init method. Once init is finished,
its accessible as ``app.openapi_schema``. As such you can always access it inside route handlers, dependencies, etc. by
access the request instance:

.. code-block:: python

   from litestar import Request, get

[4] (docs/usage/openapi/index.rst)
OpenAPI
=======

Litestar has first class OpenAPI support offering the following features:

- Automatic `OpenAPI 3.1.0 Schema <https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0>`_ generation, which is available as both YAML
  and JSON.
- Builtin support for static documentation site generation using several different libraries.
- Full configuration using pre-defined type-safe dataclasses.

Litestar includes a complete implementation of the `latest version of the OpenAPI specification <https://spec.openapis.org/oas/latest.html>`_
using Python dataclasses. This implementation is used as a basis for generating OpenAPI specs,
supporting :func:`~dataclasses.dataclass`, :class:`~typing.TypedDict`,
as well as Pydantic and msgspec models, and any 3rd party entities
for which a :ref:`plugin <plugins>` is implemented.

This is also highly configurable - and users can customize the OpenAPI spec in a variety of ways - ranging from passing
configuration globally to setting

handler decorators.

    schema_generation
    ui_plugins

[5] (docs/usage/openapi/ui_plugins.rst)
OpenAPI UI Plugins
------------------

.. versionadded:: 2.8.0

OpenAPI UI Plugins are designed to allow easy integration with your OpenAPI UI framework of choice. These plugins
facilitate the creation of interactive, user-friendly API documentation, making it easier for developers and end-users
to understand and interact with your API.

Litestar maintains and ships with UI plugins for a range of popular popular OpenAPI documentation tools:

- `Scalar <https://github.com/scalar/scalar/>`_
- `RapiDoc <https://rapidocweb.com/>`_
- `ReDoc <https://redocly.com/>`_
- `Stoplight Elements <https://stoplight.io/open-source/elements>`_
- `Swagger UI <https://swagger.io/tools/swagger-ui/>`_
- `YAML <https://yaml.org/>`_

Each plugin is easily configurable, allowing developers to customize aspects like version, paths, CSS and JavaScript
resources.

Using OpenAPI UI Plugins
------------------------

Using OpenAPI UI Plugins is as simple as importing the plugin, instantiating it, and adding it to the OpenAPIConfig.

parsed_text:
Yes, Litestar **does support** OpenAPI authentication documentation. Here's how to configure it:

Use the `OpenAPIConfig` with the `security` and `components` parameters [3]:

```python
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.openapi.config import OpenAPIConfig
from litestar.openapi.v3_1_0 import Components, SecurityScheme

app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[...],
    openapi_config=OpenAPIConfig(
        title="my api",
        version="1.0.0",
        security=[{"BearerToken": []}],  # Define security requirements
        components=Components(
            security_schemes={
                "BearerToken": SecurityScheme(
                    type="http",
                    scheme="bearer",
                )
            },
        ),
    ),
)
```

This configuration:
- **`security_schemes`** [3]: Defines the authentication schemes (e.g., Bearer token, API key, OAuth2)
- **`security`** [3]: Applies the security requirement globally to all endpoints

The generated OpenAPI schema will then document these authentication requirements, which will be visible in all the supported documentation UIs: Swagger UI, ReDoc, Stoplight Elements, RapiDoc, and Scalar [2].

[1] (docs/usage/testing.rst)
The following example uses a shared instance of an ``httpx.AsyncClient``. It uses the common factory function, which
allows to customise the client for tests, for example to add authentication headers.

.. literalinclude:: /examples/testing/async_resource_test_issue.py
    :language: python

Running this test will fail with a ``RuntimeError: Event loop is closed``, when trying to close the ``AsyncClient``
instance. This is happening because:

[2] (docs/usage/testing.rst)
- The ``http_test_client`` fixture sets up the client in *event loop A*
- The ``TestClient`` instance created within the ``test_handler`` test sets up *event loop B* and runs the application
  in it
- A call to ``http_client.get``, the ``httpx.AsyncClient`` instance creates a new connection within *loop B* and
  attaches it to the client instance
- The ``TestClient`` instance closes *event loop B*
- The cleanup step of the ``http_test_client`` fixture calls ``httpx.AsyncClient.aclose()`` instance within *loop A*,
  which internally tries to close the connection made in the previous step. That connection however is still attached
  to *loop B* that was owned by the ``TestClient`` instance, and is now closed

This can easily fixed by switching the test from :class:`~litestar.testing.TestClient` to

.. literalinclude:: /examples/testing/async_resource_test_issue_fix.py
    :language: python

Now the fixture, test and application code are all running within the same event loop, ensuring that all resources can
be cleaned up properly without issues.

[3] (docs/usage/middleware/creating-middleware.rst)
Once a middleware finishes doing whatever its doing, it should pass ``scope``, ``receive``, and ``send`` to an ASGI app
and await it. This is what's happening in the above example with: ``await self.app(scope, receive, send)``. Let's
explore another example - redirecting the request to a different url from a middleware:

.. code-block:: python

   from litestar.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

   from litestar.response.redirect import ASGIRedirectResponse
   from litestar import Request
   from litestar.middleware.base import MiddlewareProtocol

   class RedirectMiddleware(MiddlewareProtocol):
       def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp) -> None:
           self.app = app

       async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
           if Request(scope).session is None:
               response = ASGIRedirectResponse(path="/login")
               await response(scope, receive, send)
           else:
               await self.app(scope, receive, send)

[4] (docs/usage/middleware/creating-middleware.rst)
class MyRequestLoggingMiddleware(MiddlewareProtocol):
       def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp) -> None:  # can have other parameters as well
           self.app = app

       async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
           if scope["type"] == "http":
               request = Request(scope)
               logger.info("Got request: %s - %s", request.method, request.url)
           await self.app(scope, receive, send)

.. important::

    Although ``scope`` is used to create an instance of request by passing it to the
    :class:`~litestar.connection.Request` constructor, which makes it simpler to access because it does some parsing
    for you already, the actual source of truth remains ``scope`` - not the request. If you need to modify the data of
    the request you must modify the scope object, not any ephemeral request objects created as in the above.

Responding using the MiddlewareProtocol
+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

[5] (docs/usage/routing/handlers.rst)
async def my_websocket_handler(socket: WebSocket) -> None:
       await socket.accept()
       await socket.send_json({...})
       await socket.close()

    my_websocket_handler = WebsocketRouteHandler(
        path="/socket",
        fn=my_websocket_handler,
    )

In difference to HTTP routes handlers, websocket handlers have the following requirements:

#. They **must** declare a ``socket`` :term:`kwarg <argument>`.
#. They **must** have a return :term:`annotation` of ``None``.
#. They **must** be :ref:`async functions <python:async def>`.

These requirements are enforced using inspection, and if any of them is unfulfilled an informative exception
will be raised.

OpenAPI currently does not support websockets. As such no schema will be generated for these route handlers.

    * :doc:`/usage/websockets`

QUESTION:
How to share a httpx.AsyncClient() across the app?

I need to use httpx.AsyncClient() and I've read in their docs it's more optimal to have a single client that you share across the app vs creating a client each time in the route handler.

How can I share this client so that is available across all routes and then I can safely close it on app shutdown?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
> and I've read in their docs it's more optimal to have a single client that you share across the app

I am not aware of this. However, there's nothing preventing you from doing that. You can just create an instance of the client and then import that.

If you want something that's specifically bound to the lifetime of the application, you could of course use application state, dependency injection and the lifetime hooks.

```python
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.datastructures import State
from litestar.di import Provide
from httpx import AsyncClient


def on_startup(state: State) -> None:
  state.client = AsyncClient()


async def on_shutdown(state: State) -> None:
  await state.client.aclose()


def get_client(state: State) -> AsyncClient:
  return state.client


app = Litestar(
  on_startup=[on_startup],
  on_shutdown=[on_shutdown], 
  dependencies={"client": Provide(get_client)}
)
```

ANSWER B:
# Sharing httpx.AsyncClient Across FastAPI

Here are the best approaches:

## **Option 1: Using Lifespan (Recommended - FastAPI 0.93+)**

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
import httpx

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Startup
    app.state.http_client = httpx.AsyncClient()
    yield
    # Shutdown
    await app.state.http_client.aclose()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

@app.get("/")
async def my_route():
    response = await app.state.http_client.get("https://example.com")
    return response.json()
```

## **Option 2: Using Dependency Injection (Cleaner in routes)**

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
import httpx

app = FastAPI()

@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup():
    app.state.http_client = httpx.AsyncClient()

@app.on_event("shutdown")
async def shutdown():
    await app.state.http_client.aclose()

async def get_http_client() -> httpx.AsyncClient:
    return app.state.http_client

@app.get("/")
async def my_route(client: httpx.AsyncClient = Depends(get_http_client)):
    response = await client.get("https://example.com")
    return response.json()
```

## **Option 3: Simple app.state (Minimal)**

```python
@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup():
    app.state.http_client = httpx.AsyncClient()

@app.on_event("shutdown")
async def shutdown():
    await app.state.http_client.aclose()
```

Then access it anywhere as `app.state.http_client`.

---

**Recommendation:** Use **Option 1 (lifespan)** for modern FastAPI. It's cleaner and consolidates startup/shutdown logic. Use **Option 2** if you want dependency injection benefits in specific routes.

QUESTION:
Support for OpenAPI authentication documentation

The OpenAPI schema supports definining authentication and protectecd API endpoints:
https://swagger.io/docs/specification/authentication/

Now I was wondering if Litestar already supports that, because I was not able to find/activate it. So if it's supported I'd be very grateful if someone could point me out how to do it. 

Otherwise I think it would make sense to add that.

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
the `AbstractAuthenticationMiddleware` is an abstract class that needs to be implemented in a concrete way. Once you implement an authentication backend, you can configure your openapi specs to include authentication documentation for this. 

An example of how to do this can be found here: https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/main/litestar/contrib/jwt/jwt_auth.py#L68

There is no way for us to automagically know what you implemented. 


ANSWER A:
# Litestar OpenAPI Authentication Support

Yes, **Litestar does support OpenAPI authentication**, but the documentation can be sparse. Here's how to configure it:

## Basic Setup

Define security schemes at the application level:

```python
from litestar import Litestar, get
from litestar.security.jwt import JWTAuth
from litestar.openapi.config import OpenAPIConfig

jwt_auth = JWTAuth(algorithm="HS256", token_secret="your-secret-key")

@get("/protected", sync_to_thread=False)
def protected_route() -> dict:
    return {"message": "authenticated"}

app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[protected_route],
    openapi_config=OpenAPIConfig(title="My API", version="1.0.0"),
    security=[jwt_auth],  # Apply globally
)
```

## Apply to Specific Routes

```python
from litestar import Litestar, get
from litestar.security.jwt import JWTAuth
from litestar.status_codes import HTTP_200_OK

jwt_auth = JWTAuth(algorithm="HS256", token_secret="your-secret-key")

@get("/protected", security=[jwt_auth])
def protected_route() -> dict:
    return {"message": "authenticated"}
```

## Other Authentication Methods

Litestar supports multiple authentication schemes:
- **JWT** - `JWTAuth`
- **API Key** - `APIKeyAuth`
- **Basic Auth** - `BasicAuth`
- **Bearer Token** - Custom via `AbstractSecurityConfig`

## OpenAPI Documentation

Once configured, the security scheme will automatically appear in your OpenAPI schema (`/schema/openapi.json`). It should show up in the `securitySchemes` section.

**If you're not seeing it in the schema**, check that:
1. Your security handlers are properly instantiated
2. Routes have the `security` parameter applied
3. OpenAPI config is properly initialized

Could you share your current Litestar version? Recent versions (0.40+) have improved security documentation handling.

ANSWER B:

Use the `OpenAPIConfig` with the `security` and `components` parameters [3]:

```python
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.openapi.config import OpenAPIConfig
from litestar.openapi.v3_1_0 import Components, SecurityScheme

app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[...],
    openapi_config=OpenAPIConfig(
        title="my api",
        version="1.0.0",
        security=[{"BearerToken": []}],  # Define security requirements
        components=Components(
            security_schemes={
                "BearerToken": SecurityScheme(
                    type="http",
                    scheme="bearer",
                )
            },
        ),
    ),
)
```

This configuration:
- **`security_schemes`** [3]: Defines the authentication schemes (e.g., Bearer token, API key, OAuth2)
- **`security`** [3]: Applies the security requirement globally to all endpoints

The generated OpenAPI schema will then document these authentication requirements, which will be visible in all the supported documentation UIs: Swagger UI, ReDoc, Stoplight Elements, RapiDoc, and Scalar [2].

prompt:
How do you handle settings?

How do you handle loading settings from ENV and then making them available in the route handlers?

At the moment I'm using a BaseSettings class from pydantic. Is this the right way? And how do I make this available in all the route handlers that need it?
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":8274,"duration_api_ms":9382,"ttft_ms":2707,"ttft_stream_ms":1001,"time_to_request_ms":9,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Settings with Pydantic BaseSettings

Yes, **Pydantic's `BaseSettings` is the right approach**. Here's the standard pattern:

## 1. Define Settings

```python
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    database_url: str
    api_key: str
    debug: bool = False
    
    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

settings = Settings()
```

## 2. Inject into Route Handlers (FastAPI)

Use FastAPI's dependency injection:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends

app = FastAPI()

def get_settings() -> Settings:
    return settings

@app.get("/example")
async def my_route(settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings)):
    return {"api_key": settings.api_key}
```

## 3. Simpler: Direct Singleton Access

Or just import the singleton directly in your routes:

```python
from .config import settings

@app.get("/example")
async def my_route():
    return {"debug": settings.debug}
```

## Best Practice

For larger apps, use dependency injection for **testability**:

```python
# config.py
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    database_url: str
    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

settings = Settings()

# routes.py
@app.get("/")
async def root(settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings)):
    # Can inject test settings in tests
    return {"db": settings.database_url}
```

# Settings with Pydantic BaseSettings

Yes, **Pydantic's `BaseSettings` is the right approach**. Here's the standard pattern:

## 1. Define Settings

```python
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    database_url: str
    api_key: str
    debug: bool = False
    
    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

settings = Settings()
```

## 2. Inject into Route Handlers (FastAPI)

Use FastAPI's dependency injection:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends

app = FastAPI()

def get_settings() -> Settings:
    return settings

@app.get("/example")
async def my_route(settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings)):
    return {"api_key": settings.api_key}
```

## 3. Simpler: Direct Singleton Access

Or just import the singleton directly in your routes:

```python
from .config import settings

@app.get("/example")
async def my_route():
    return {"debug": settings.debug}
```

## Best Practice

For larger apps, use dependency injection for **testability**:

```python
# config.py
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    database_url: str
    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

settings = Settings()

# routes.py
@app.get("/")
async def root(settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings)):
    # Can inject test settings in tests
    return {"db": settings.database_url}
```

**Environment variables** are automatically loaded from your `.env` file or system environment. Pydantic handles the validation and type coercion automatically.

HTTP route handlers
-------------------

The :class:`~.handlers.HTTPRouteHandler` is used to handle HTTP requests, and can be
created with the :func:`~.handlers.route` :term:`decorator`:

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Defining a route handler by decorating a function with the :func:`@route() <.handlers.route>`
      :term:`decorator`

    from litestar import HttpMethod, route

    @route(path="/some-path", http_method=[HttpMethod.GET, HttpMethod.POST])
    async def my_endpoint() -> None: ...

The same can be achieved without a decorator, by using ``HTTPRouteHandler`` directly:

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Defining a route handler creating an instance of
      :class:`HTTPRouteHandler <.handlers.HTTPRouteHandler>`

    from litestar import HttpMethod
    from litestar.handlers.http_handlers import HTTPRouteHandler

    async def my_endpoint() -> None: ...

# Setting Custom Operation Names in Litestar Schema

The `name` parameter sets the route name for URL reversal, not the schema operation name. To customize the operation name in the OpenAPI schema documentation, use one of these approaches:

## 1. **Use the `summary` parameter** (recommended for display name)
```python
@get(path="/urls", summary="URLs")
def urls(...):
    pass
```

## 2. **Use the `operation_id` parameter** (for the operationId field)
```python
@get(path="/urls", operation_id="list_urls")
def urls(...):
    pass
```

## 3. **Use the docstring** (for description/summary)
```python
@get(path="/urls")
def urls(...):
    """URLs endpoint for retrieving all URLs"""
    pass
```

## Combined Example
```python
@get(
    path="/urls",
    name="urls_route",           # for URL reversal
    summary="URLs",              # display name in docs
    operation_id="list_urls",    # OpenAPI operationId
)
def urls(...):
    """Retrieve all URLs"""
    pass
```

The **`summary` parameter** is what controls the friendly operation name shown in most API documentation viewers (Swagger UI, ReDoc, etc.). Use `operation_id` if you need to explicitly control the operationId field in the OpenAPI spec.

prompt:
You are grading two answers to a developer's question about the tool "litestar".

QUESTION:
How to define "endpoint" without using "decorator syntax" ?

An starlette example,

```python
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

app = Starlette(routes=[
        Mount(root_path, routes=[
            Route(path='/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET','POST'], name='mail'),
        ])
    ]
)
```

This way we can have central control on the route connections and easy to configure.

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
The issue you're having is that you want to do this

> This way we can have central control on the route connections and easy to configure.

but arrived at the conclusion that the way to achieve it is to not use decorators.

This conclusion however is wrong, since what you want is already how Litestar works.

The equivalent of this Starlette app

```python
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

app = Starlette(routes=[Route(path='/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET'], name='mail')])
```

is this Litestar app:

```python
@get("/{token:uuid}", name="mail")
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

app = Litestar(route_handlers=[mail_page])
```

So to answer your question: You can't. Decorators are how you define the route handlers. 
But you also don't *need* to, since the decorators are not registering the route handlers at the same time. These are strictly separated in Litestar.

The sanic example you provided is *not* equivalent to what's happening in Litestar.

```python
@app.route("/test", methods=["GET")
async def handler(request):
  ...
```

in this Sanic example, `@app.route` does two things: It creates an endpoint function, and *registers it on the application*, which, going off your initial post here is what you want avoid. But this is not what's happening with Litestar's decorators. Notice how in the Sanic example `.route` is a **method on the application instance**. In Litestar, this is not the case. Decorating a function with a route handler decorator such as `@post` does not do anything besides creating a route handler. It does not register it, since there's no application associated with it.


You can even make the Litestar and Starlette equivalents look almost identical if you really want to:

```python
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

mail_page = Route(path='/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET'], name='mail')

app = Starlette(routes=[mail_page])
```

is this Litestar app:

```python

async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

mail_page = get("/{token:uuid}", name="mail")(mail_page)

app = Litestar(route_handlers=[mail_page])
```

It's simply a difference in syntax, not in functionality.

ANSWER B:
# Defining Endpoints Without Decorator Syntax in Starlette

Your example is the correct approach! Here's a complete working pattern:

```python
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from starlette.routing import Route, Mount
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import HTMLResponse

# Define endpoint functions
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    token = request.path_params['token']
    return HTMLResponse(f"Mail page for token: {token}")

async def list_mails(request: Request):
    return HTMLResponse("Mail list")

# Central route configuration
routes = [
    Mount('/mail', routes=[
        Route('/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET', 'POST'], name='mail'),
        Route('/', endpoint=list_mails, methods=['GET'], name='mail_list'),
    ])
]

app = Starlette(routes=routes)
```

## Key Advantages of This Approach:

✅ **Centralized route management** - All routes in one place  
✅ **Easy configuration** - Modify routes without touching endpoint functions  
✅ **Named routes** - Use `request.url_for('mail', token=token_value)`  
✅ **Modular** - Split routes into separate files and combine them

## Modular Pattern Example:

```python
# routes/mail.py
from starlette.routing import Route, Mount
from .handlers import mail_page, list_mails

mail_routes = Mount('/mail', routes=[
    Route('/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET', 'POST'], name='mail'),
    Route('/', endpoint=list_mails, methods=['GET'], name='mail_list'),
])

# main.py
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from routes.mail import mail_routes

app = Starlette(routes=[mail_routes])
```

This is indeed a cleaner, more maintainable approach for larger applications.

prompt:
How do you get access to the cache from an JWTAuthenticationMiddleware?

In the docs I see it being accessed using `scope["app"].cache` but the authenticate_request of JWTAuthenticationMiddleware does not have a scope param?
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":6525,"duration_api_ms":8302,"ttft_ms":4157,"ttft_stream_ms":1212,"time_to_request_ms":7,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Accessing Cache in JWTAuthenticationMiddleware

You need to pass `scope` to your `authenticate_request` method. The middleware receives `scope` in its `__call__` method, but you must explicitly pass it along.

## Solution

Modify your `authenticate_request` signature to accept `scope`:

```python
class JWTAuthenticationMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        await self.authenticate_request(request, request.scope)
        return await call_next(request)
```

# Accessing Cache in JWTAuthenticationMiddleware

You need to pass `scope` to your `authenticate_request` method. The middleware receives `scope` in its `__call__` method, but you must explicitly pass it along.

## Solution

Modify your `authenticate_request` signature to accept `scope`:

```python
class JWTAuthenticationMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        await self.authenticate_request(request, request.scope)
        return await call_next(request)
    
    async def authenticate_request(self, request: Request, scope):
        cache = scope["app"].cache
        # use cache...
```

Or more directly via the request object:

```python
async def authenticate_request(self, request: Request):
    cache = request.app.cache
    # use cache...
```

## Key Points

- The `scope` dict is available in the middleware's `dispatch` method
- You can pass it to `authenticate_request`, or access it via `request.app`
- `request.app` is a shorthand for `request.scope["app"]` (the application instance)
- Both approaches give you access to any attributes on your app, including `cache`

The second approach (`request.app.cache`) is cleaner since you already have the request object available.

parsed_text:
# Getting Client IP in Starlite

You can access the client IP directly from the `Request` object in your HTTP handler—no middleware needed. Here's how:

```python
from starlite import Request, get

@get("/")
async def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    client_ip = request.client.host if request.client else None
    return {"client_ip": client_ip}
```

## For Proxied Requests

If you're behind a reverse proxy (nginx, load balancer, etc.), also check the `X-Forwarded-For` header:

```python
from starlite import Request, get

@get("/")
async def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    x_forwarded_for = request.headers.get("x-forwarded-for")
    if x_forwarded_for:
        client_ip = x_forwarded_for.split(",")[0].strip()
    else:
        client_ip = request.client.host if request.client else None
    return {"client_ip": client_ip}
```

The `request.client` attribute returns a tuple of `(host, port)` (or `None` if unavailable), so you access the IP with `.host`.

This is the documented approach and should work in any HTTP handler where you inject the `Request` parameter.

and can simply be added to the ``route_handlers`` like any other regular handler.

Usage of ``url_for_static_assets`` should be replaced with a ``url_for("static", ...)``
call.

Implicit Optional Default Parameters
------------------------------------

In v2, if a handler was typed with an optional parameter it would be implicitly given a default value of ``None``. For
example, if the following handler is called with no query parameter, the value ``None`` would be passed in to the
handler for the ``param`` parameter:

.. code-block:: python

    @get("/")
    def my_handler(param: int | None) -> ...:
        ...

This legacy behavior originates from our history of using Pydantic v1 models to represent handler signatures. In v3, we
no longer make this implicit conversion. If you want to have a default value of ``None`` for an optional parameter, you
must explicitly set it:

.. code-block:: python

    @get("/")
    def my_handler(param: int | None = None) -> ...:
        ...

[2] (docs/usage/routing/overview.rst)
@get(["/", "/sub-path"])
    def handler() -> None: ...

    app = Litestar(route_handlers=[handler])

To handle more complex path schemas you should use :class:`controllers <.controller.Controller>` and

Registering routes dynamically
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Occasionally there is a need for dynamic route registration. Litestar supports this via the

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Registering a route handler dynamically with the :paramref:`~.app.Litestar.register` method

    from litestar import Litestar, get

    @get()
    def root_handler() -> None: ...

    app = Litestar(route_handlers=[root_handler])

    @get("/sub-path")
    def sub_path_handler() -> None: ...

    app.register(sub_path_handler)

Since the app instance is attached to all instances of :class:`~.connection.base.ASGIConnection`,

effect call the :meth:`~.router.Router.register` method inside route handler functions, middlewares, and even
injected dependencies. For example:

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Call the :meth:`~.router.Router.register` method from inside a route handler function

Customizing Pydantic model schemas
----------------------------------

You can customize the OpenAPI schemas generated for pydantic models by following the guidelines in
the `Pydantic docs <https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/usage/json_schema/>`_.

Additionally, you can affect how pydantic models are translated into OpenAPI ``components`` by settings a special dunder
attribute on the model called ``__schema_name__``:

.. literalinclude:: /examples/openapi/customize_pydantic_model_name.py

The above will result in an OpenAPI schema object that looks like this:

# Common Swagger/OpenAPI Documentation URLs

Without knowing your specific framework, here are common endpoints to try:

- `/swagger-ui/` or `/swagger-ui.html`
- `/api/docs`
- `/apidocs`
- `/swagger/`
- `/openapi.json` (the spec file itself)

## If you're using FastAPI:
- `/docs` (Swagger UI) - requires `from fastapi import FastAPI`
- `/redoc` (ReDoc alternative)
- If these don't work, check that Swagger is enabled in your app setup

## To help you better, please clarify:
1. **What framework are you using?** (FastAPI, Flask, Django, Spring, etc.)
2. **Can you share your app initialization code?**
3. **What does `curl localhost:8000/openapi.json` return?** (This checks if OpenAPI schema is being generated)

If Swagger isn't showing up, it might not be enabled. For example, in FastAPI you need the standard setup with dependencies installed (`fastapi[all]`).

class MyRequestLoggingMiddleware(MiddlewareProtocol):
       def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp) -> None:  # can have other parameters as well
           self.app = app

       async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
           if scope["type"] == "http":
               request = Request(scope)
               logger.info("Got request: %s - %s", request.method, request.url)
           await self.app(scope, receive, send)

.. important::

    Although ``scope`` is used to create an instance of request by passing it to the
    :class:`~litestar.connection.Request` constructor, which makes it simpler to access because it does some parsing
    for you already, the actual source of truth remains ``scope`` - not the request. If you need to modify the data of
    the request you must modify the scope object, not any ephemeral request objects created as in the above.

QUESTION:
How do you handle settings?

How do you handle loading settings from ENV and then making them available in the route handlers?

At the moment I'm using a BaseSettings class from pydantic. Is this the right way? And how do I make this available in all the route handlers that need it?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
We have created an `AppSettings` class based off `BaseSettings`.  We then use dependency injection to make the settings available.

*`config.py`*
```python
class AppSettings(BaseSettings):
    app_name: str = "My Awesome App"
    app_short_name: str = "awesome"
    admin_email: str = "foo@bar.com"
    mongodb_url: str
    mongodb_db: str
```

*`dependencies.py`*
```python
@lru_cache()
def get_settings() -> AppSettings:
    return AppSettings()
```

*`main.py`*
```python
app = Starlite(
    ...
    dependencies={
        "settings": Provide(get_settings, use_cache=True),
    },
)
```

Within a given controller, we can say something like:

```python
async def my_endpoint(
    self,
    settings: AppSettings,
)
```

...and the `settings` variable holds our app settings.

Since this is based off Pydantic's `BaseSettings` we can use a mixture of default / hard-coded, environment variables, and `.env` files.

https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/dependency-injection.html

https://docs.pydantic.dev/usage/settings/


ANSWER A:
# Settings with Pydantic BaseSettings

Yes, **Pydantic's `BaseSettings` is the right approach**. Here's the standard pattern:

## 1. Define Settings

```python
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    database_url: str
    api_key: str
    debug: bool = False
    
    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

settings = Settings()
```

## 2. Inject into Route Handlers (FastAPI)

Use FastAPI's dependency injection:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends

app = FastAPI()

def get_settings() -> Settings:
    return settings

@app.get("/example")
async def my_route(settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings)):
    return {"api_key": settings.api_key}
```

## 3. Simpler: Direct Singleton Access

Or just import the singleton directly in your routes:

```python
from .config import settings

@app.get("/example")
async def my_route():
    return {"debug": settings.debug}
```

## Best Practice

For larger apps, use dependency injection for **testability**:

```python
# config.py
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    database_url: str
    class Config:
        env_file = ".env"

settings = Settings()

# routes.py
@app.get("/")
async def root(settings: Settings = Depends(get_settings)):
    # Can inject test settings in tests
    return {"db": settings.database_url}
```

**Environment variables** are automatically loaded from your `.env` file or system environment. Pydantic handles the validation and type coercion automatically.

Is there a specific framework you're using, or do you need help with the `.env` file setup?

ANSWER B:
I don't have enough information to answer your question about settings management in Litestar. The provided documentation context doesn't cover how to load settings from environment variables or make them available to route handlers.

To get a proper answer, you would need to consult:
- The Litestar documentation on dependency injection
- The Litestar documentation on application configuration
- Examples showing how to integrate Pydantic's `BaseSettings` (or modern `pydantic_settings`) with Litestar's dependency system

I'd recommend checking the official Litestar documentation site for sections on "Configuration," "Settings," or "Dependency Injection" to see the recommended patterns for your use case.

Returning responses from third party libraries
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Because you can return any ASGI Application from a route handler, you can also use any ASGI application from other
libraries. For example, you can return the response classes from Starlette or FastAPI directly from route handlers:

.. code-block:: python

   from starlette.responses import JSONResponse

   from litestar import get
   from litestar.types import ASGIApp

   @get("/")
   def handler() -> ASGIApp:
       return JSONResponse(content={"hello": "world"})  # type: ignore

Question: Documentation: correct way to get client IP?

FastAPI has a [simple mechanism for accessing client information](https://stackoverflow.com/a/66015976) from the `Request` object. It looks like this information is stored in `ASGIConnection` in Starlite, but it's not clear to me how to access this object from a HTTP handler from the documentation, only middleware.

If this is easily done can an example be added to the documentation? Let me know if I'm missing something simple.
parsed_text:
# Getting Client IP in Litestar

You can access the client IP directly from a `Request` object injected into your HTTP handler. Since `Request` is a subclass of `ASGIConnection`, you can use the `.client` property [5]:

```python
from litestar import get
from litestar.connection import Request

@get("/")
def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    client_ip = request.client[0]  # First element is the host IP
    client_port = request.client[1]  # Second element is the port
    return {"client_ip": client_ip}
```

According to the documentation, `ASGIConnection.client()` [5] "Return the ``client`` data of this connection's ``Scope``" and returns "A two tuple of the host name and port number."

Alternatively, you can use the `ConnectionDataExtractor` utility [4]:

```python
from litestar.data_extractors import ConnectionDataExtractor
from litestar import get
from litestar.connection import Request

@get("/")
def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    client_data = ConnectionDataExtractor.extract_client(request)
    return {"client": client_data}
```

The `.client` property approach is simpler and more direct for standard use cases.

Semantic handler :term:`decorators <decorator>`
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Litestar also includes "semantic" :term:`decorators <decorator>`, that is, :term:`decorators <decorator>` the pre-set
the :paramref:`~litestar.handlers.HTTPRouteHandler.http_method` :term:`kwarg <argument>` to a specific HTTP verb,
which correlates with their name:

* :func:`@delete() <.handlers.delete>`
* :func:`@get() <.handlers.get>`
* :func:`@head() <.handlers.head>`
* :func:`@patch() <.handlers.patch>`
* :func:`@post() <.handlers.post>`
* :func:`@put() <.handlers.put>`

These are used exactly like :func:`@route() <.handlers.route>` with the sole exception that you don't need to configure
the :paramref:`~.handlers.HTTPRouteHandler.http_method` :term:`kwarg <argument>`:

.. dropdown:: Click to see the predefined route handlers

    .. code-block:: python
        :caption: Predefined :term:`decorators <decorator>` for HTTP route handlers

[2] (docs/release-notes/whats-new-3.rst)
As a result, customizing the decorators directly is not possible anymore. Instead, to
use a route handler decorator with a custom route handler class, the ``handler_class``
parameter to the decorator function can be used:

Before:

.. code-block:: python

    class my_get_handler(get):
        ... # custom handler

    @my_get_handler()
    async def handler() -> Any:
        ...

After:

.. code-block:: python

    class MyHTTPRouteHandler(HTTPRouteHandler):
        ... # custom handler

    @get(handler_class=MyHTTPRouteHandler)
    async def handler() -> Any:
        ...

How do you get access to the cache from an JWTAuthenticationMiddleware?

In the docs I see it being accessed using `scope["app"].cache` but the authenticate_request of JWTAuthenticationMiddleware does not have a scope param?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
> but the authenticate_request of JWTAuthenticationMiddleware does not have a scope param?

`scope` is an attribute of `ASGIConnection`, which is passed into `authenticate_request`.
So you could access the scope like this: `connection.scope`, and get the cache with `connection.scope["app"].cache`.

You don't need to go through so many indirections though, as `ASGIConenction` directly exposes `app` and `cache` properties, which means the easiest way to access it is `connection.cache`.

Check out the [`ASGIConnection` docs](https://starliteproject.dev/lib/reference/connection/base.html#starlite.connection.base.ASGIConnection).

ANSWER A:
# Accessing Cache in JWTAuthenticationMiddleware

You need to pass `scope` to your `authenticate_request` method. The middleware receives `scope` in its `__call__` method, but you must explicitly pass it along.

## Solution

Modify your `authenticate_request` signature to accept `scope`:

```python
class JWTAuthenticationMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        await self.authenticate_request(request, request.scope)
        return await call_next(request)
    
    async def authenticate_request(self, request: Request, scope):
        cache = scope["app"].cache
        # use cache...
```

Or more directly via the request object:

```python
async def authenticate_request(self, request: Request):
    cache = request.app.cache
    # use cache...
```

## Key Points

- The `scope` dict is available in the middleware's `dispatch` method
- You can pass it to `authenticate_request`, or access it via `request.app`
- `request.app` is a shorthand for `request.scope["app"]` (the application instance)
- Both approaches give you access to any attributes on your app, including `cache`

The second approach (`request.app.cache`) is cleaner since you already have the request object available.

ANSWER B:

However, the provided docs don't explain how to access the app or cache through the `connection` object.

The example in [5] shows accessing `scope["app"]` in standard middleware, but that's a different context where `scope` is directly available to the middleware's `__call__` method.

To get a definitive answer on how to access `scope["app"].cache` from within `JWTAuthenticationMiddleware.authenticate_request()`, you would need to:
- Check if `connection` has an `app` attribute or similar that provides access to the application instance
- Consult the Litestar HTTPConnection API documentation

QUESTION:
Changing operation names for handlers in the schema

Maybe I am looking in the wrong place for this functionality. By default, HTTP-method decorated route handlers are given title-case converted names in the schema documentation. For some handlers, the title casing is not ideal, or otherwise one might want a name that is not necessarily the same as the name of the function.

My expectation would be that the name parameter passed to the decorator would override the default name, but that does not seem to be the case. Is there a way to change the operation name in the docs for an operation associated with a handler?

e.g., I am expecting this would override "Urls" in the docs to being "URLs":

```
@get(path="/urls", name="URLs")
def urls( ...
```

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
Oh, I found it. The parameter is `operation_id`. So, for future reference, this should work:

```
@get(path="/urls", operation_id="URLs")
def urls( ...
```

ANSWER A:
Based on the provided documentation, there isn't a direct parameter to override the operation name/ID shown in your example.

However, the documentation [3] shows that you can use the **`operation_class`** parameter to fully customize the operation object:

```python
@get(path="/urls", operation_class=MyCustomOperation)
def urls(...):
    ...
```

This parameter accepts "a subclass of :class:`Operation <.openapi.spec.operation.Operation>` which can be used to fully customize the `operation object`" for the handler [3], which would include the `operationId`.

The default behavior uses a `default_operation_id_creator` function [4] that generates camelCased operation IDs from the handler function name, HTTP method, and path components. By subclassing `Operation`, you could override how the operationId is generated.

**However**, the provided documentation doesn't include details on how to implement this custom `Operation` subclass. You may need to consult additional documentation or the library's source code for the complete implementation details on customizing the `operationId` through `operation_class`.

ANSWER B:
# Setting Custom Operation Names in Litestar Schema

The `name` parameter sets the route name for URL reversal, not the schema operation name. To customize the operation name in the OpenAPI schema documentation, use one of these approaches:

## 1. **Use the `summary` parameter** (recommended for display name)
```python
@get(path="/urls", summary="URLs")
def urls(...):
    pass
```

## 2. **Use the `operation_id` parameter** (for the operationId field)
```python
@get(path="/urls", operation_id="list_urls")
def urls(...):
    pass
```

## 3. **Use the docstring** (for description/summary)
```python
@get(path="/urls")
def urls(...):
    """URLs endpoint for retrieving all URLs"""
    pass
```

## Combined Example
```python
@get(
    path="/urls",
    name="urls_route",           # for URL reversal
    summary="URLs",              # display name in docs
    operation_id="list_urls",    # OpenAPI operationId
)
def urls(...):
    """Retrieve all URLs"""
    pass
```

The **`summary` parameter** is what controls the friendly operation name shown in most API documentation viewers (Swagger UI, ReDoc, etc.). Use `operation_id` if you need to explicitly control the operationId field in the OpenAPI spec.

QUESTION:
Documentation: correct way to get client IP?

FastAPI has a [simple mechanism for accessing client information](https://stackoverflow.com/a/66015976) from the `Request` object. It looks like this information is stored in `ASGIConnection` in Starlite, but it's not clear to me how to access this object from a HTTP handler from the documentation, only middleware.

If this is easily done can an example be added to the documentation? Let me know if I'm missing something simple.

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
This is not FastAPI specific. This info is part of the ASGI spec and is as such accessible as part of the ASGI scope dictionary. 

You can access `request.client` to access it. Simply have the request instance injected into the route handler (or the websocket instance) to interact with it. 

ANSWER A:
# Getting Client IP in Litestar

You can access the client IP directly from a `Request` object injected into your HTTP handler. Since `Request` is a subclass of `ASGIConnection`, you can use the `.client` property [5]:

```python
from litestar import get
from litestar.connection import Request

@get("/")
def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    client_ip = request.client[0]  # First element is the host IP
    client_port = request.client[1]  # Second element is the port
    return {"client_ip": client_ip}
```

According to the documentation, `ASGIConnection.client()` [5] "Return the ``client`` data of this connection's ``Scope``" and returns "A two tuple of the host name and port number."

Alternatively, you can use the `ConnectionDataExtractor` utility [4]:

```python
from litestar.data_extractors import ConnectionDataExtractor
from litestar import get
from litestar.connection import Request

@get("/")
def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    client_data = ConnectionDataExtractor.extract_client(request)
    return {"client": client_data}
```

The `.client` property approach is simpler and more direct for standard use cases.

ANSWER B:
# Getting Client IP in Starlite

You can access the client IP directly from the `Request` object in your HTTP handler—no middleware needed. Here's how:

```python
from starlite import Request, get

@get("/")
async def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    client_ip = request.client.host if request.client else None
    return {"client_ip": client_ip}
```

## For Proxied Requests

If you're behind a reverse proxy (nginx, load balancer, etc.), also check the `X-Forwarded-For` header:

```python
from starlite import Request, get

@get("/")
async def handler(request: Request) -> dict:
    x_forwarded_for = request.headers.get("x-forwarded-for")
    if x_forwarded_for:
        client_ip = x_forwarded_for.split(",")[0].strip()
    else:
        client_ip = request.client.host if request.client else None
    return {"client_ip": client_ip}
```

The `request.client` attribute returns a tuple of `(host, port)` (or `None` if unavailable), so you access the IP with `.host`.

This is the documented approach and should work in any HTTP handler where you inject the `Request` parameter.

The issue is that `Response(content=...)` expects **bytes**, not a `BytesIO` object. Change:

```python
from starlite import get, Response
import io

@get(
    "randompic/{lang:str}",
    media_type='image/jpeg'
```

# Fix: Use `buf.getvalue()` to get the bytes

The issue is that `Response(content=...)` expects **bytes**, not a `BytesIO` object. Change:

```python
from starlite import get, Response
import io

@get(
    "randompic/{lang:str}",
    media_type='image/jpeg'
)
def randompic(lang: str) -> Response:
    img = rdpgen.generate_random_definition_pic(lang)
    
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    img.save(buf, format="JPEG")
    
    return Response(content=buf.getvalue(), media_type="image/jpeg")
```

**Key changes:**
1. `buf.getvalue()` extracts the bytes instead of passing the BytesIO object
2. Return type is `Response` (not `io.BytesIO`)
3. No need for `buf.seek(0)` when using `getvalue()`

Alternatively, if you prefer a more concise approach without the intermediate buffer variable:

```python
@get("randompic/{lang:str}", media_type='image/jpeg')
def randompic(lang: str) -> Response:
    img = rdpgen.generate_random_definition_pic(lang)
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    img.save(buf, format="JPEG")
    return Response(content=buf.getvalue(), media_type="image/jpeg")
```

This should resolve the serialization error.

prompt:
Is there a way to integrate tortoise-orm instead of sqlalchemy

I would like to use starlite, but it is tightly integrated with sqlalchemy. Sqlalchemy pulls in greenlet, greenlet lot of issues with M1 processor. It has almost become a no go just due to sqlachemy integration. We like everything it offers but the ORM!
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":6460,"duration_api_ms":7660,"ttft_ms":3151,"ttft_stream_ms":776,"time_to_request_ms":6,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Using Tortoise-ORM with Starlite/Litestar

The good news: **you can use Starlite/Litestar without its SQLAlchemy integration**. The ORM support is optional, not required.

## Recommended approach:

1. **Skip the built-in ORM integration** entirely
2. **Use tortoise-orm independently** in your application
3. **Manage the lifecycle** yourself in startup/shutdown handlers

## Basic example:

```python
from litestar import Litestar, get
from tortoise import Tortoise

@get("/")
async def hello() -> dict:
    return {"message": "hello"}

async def init_db():
    await Tortoise.init(
        db_url='sqlite://:memory:',
        modules={'models': ['app.models']}
    )
    await Tortoise.generate_schemas()

async def close_db():
    await Tortoise.close_connections()

app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[hello],
    on_startup=[init_db],
    on_shutdown=[close_db],
)
```

## Key points:

- Don't use Starlite's `SQLAlchemyConfig` or related features
- Use Litestar (the newer maintained version, formerly Starlite)
- Tortoise-orm is async-first, which integrates naturally with Litestar's async patterns
- This avoids the greenlet dependency entirely

## Alternative:

# Using Tortoise-ORM with Starlite/Litestar

The good news: **you can use Starlite/Litestar without its SQLAlchemy integration**. The ORM support is optional, not required.

## Recommended approach:

1. **Skip the built-in ORM integration** entirely
2. **Use tortoise-orm independently** in your application
3. **Manage the lifecycle** yourself in startup/shutdown handlers

## Basic example:

```python
from litestar import Litestar, get
from tortoise import Tortoise

@get("/")
async def hello() -> dict:
    return {"message": "hello"}

async def init_db():
    await Tortoise.init(
        db_url='sqlite://:memory:',
        modules={'models': ['app.models']}
    )
    await Tortoise.generate_schemas()

async def close_db():
    await Tortoise.close_connections()

app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[hello],
    on_startup=[init_db],
    on_shutdown=[close_db],
)
```

## Key points:

- Don't use Starlite's `SQLAlchemyConfig` or related features
- Use Litestar (the newer maintained version, formerly Starlite)
- Tortoise-orm is async-first, which integrates naturally with Litestar's async patterns
- This avoids the greenlet dependency entirely

## Alternative:

If you need built-in ORM features (plugins, dependency injection helpers), you could open an issue/PR with the Litestar community to add tortoise-orm support, but the standalone approach above is solid and gives you full control.

prompt:
How To Handle Path Parameters of Multiple Valid Types

We have endpoints where a path parameter can be multiple types (string, integer, boolean).  These are normally used for filtering.  For example:

```
class Widget(Document):
    name: str
    some_bool: bool
    some_str: str
    some_int: int


class WidgetByAttr(str, Enum):
    # Set of widget attributes that we allow filtering on
    some_bool = "some_bool"
    some_str = "some_str"
    some_int = "some_int"


@get(
    path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",
)
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: Union[int, bool, str],
) -> List[Widget]:
    widgets = []
    for widget in await Widget.find(
        getattr(Widget, widget_attr) == attr_value
    ).to_list(length=100):
        widgets.append(widget)

    return widgets
```

The data type specified for `attr_value` in the `path` seems to be overriding the function signature (`Union[...]`).  In other words, `attr_value` is always coerced to a string (never int or bool).

Thoughts?
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":10204,"duration_api_ms":11003,"ttft_ms":9626,"ttft_stream_ms":2292,"time_to_request_ms":7,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Path Parameters Are Always Strings

The core issue: **HTTP path parameters are always strings**. When you specify `:str` in the path, you're being explicit about this, but it would be strings regardless. The function signature's `Union[int, bool, str]` has no effect on path parameter parsing.

## Solution: Convert in the Handler

Do the type conversion inside your function based on the attribute being filtered:

```python
@get(
    path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",
)
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: str,
) -> List[Widget]:
    # Get the field and its type from the model
    field = getattr(Widget, widget_attr)
    field_type = Widget.model_fields[widget_attr].annotation
    
    # Convert attr_value to the correct type
    if field_type == bool:
        converted_value = attr_value.lower() in ('true', '1', 'yes')
    elif field_type == int:
        converted_value = int(attr_value)
    else:
        converted_value = attr_value
    
    widgets = await Widget.find(
        field == converted_value
    ).to_list(length=100)
    
    return widgets
```

## Alternative: Use Query Parameters

If flexibility is important, consider query parameters instead:

```python
@get(
    path="/widgets/by-attr",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",
)
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: str,
) -> List[Widget]:
    # Same conversion logic as above...
```

Then call it as: `/widgets/by-attr?widget_attr=some_int&attr_value=42`

```python

    path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",
)
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: str,
) -> List[Widget]:
    # Get the field and its type from the model
    field = getattr(Widget, widget_attr)
    field_type = Widget.model_fields[widget_attr].annotation
    
    # Convert attr_value to the correct type
    if field_type == bool:
        converted_value = attr_value.lower() in ('true', '1', 'yes')
    elif field_type == int:
        converted_value = int(attr_value)
    else:

    return widgets
```

## Alternative: Use Query Parameters

If flexibility is important, consider query parameters instead:

```python
@get(
    path="/widgets/by-attr",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",

async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
```

Then call it as: `/widgets/by-attr?widget_attr=some_int&attr_value=42`

prompt:
how to show exception traces?

How do I get the server to show my application exceptions in development? I guess I figured that's what the debug flag is for, but it still just logs out a 500 when debug is enabled.
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":7103,"duration_api_ms":8204,"ttft_ms":4142,"ttft_stream_ms":804,"time_to_request_ms":6,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Showing Exception Traces

The solution depends on your framework, but here are common approaches:

## Flask (Python)
```python
# Set environment variables
export FLASK_ENV=development
export FLASK_DEBUG=1
```

Or in code:
```python
app = Flask(__name__)
app.config['DEBUG'] = True
app.config['PROPAGATE_EXCEPTIONS'] = True
```

When debug is enabled, Flask's Werkzeug debugger should display full tracebacks in the browser.

## Django (Python)
```python
# settings.py
DEBUG = True
```

Full tracebacks appear in the browser and console.

## General Troubleshooting

If you're still seeing generic 500 errors:

1. **Check if exceptions are being caught** — Look for try/except blocks that might be swallowing errors:
```python
try:
    # risky code
except:
    return "Error"  # ❌ This hides the actual exception
```

2. **Check application logs** — The actual traceback might be in logs:
```bash
tail -f app.log
# or check your framework's log output
```

3. **Add explicit error handling** to see traces:
```python
@app.errorhandler(500)
def handle_error(e):
    return str(e), 500  # Return the actual exception
```

4. **Check the console** — Server output may show the traceback even if the browser doesn't.

prompt:
Common response wrappers

Hello, is there a way to wrap handlers responses (JSON/HTTP) with a generic schema? 
Can this feature work also in the OpenAPI schema generation?

For example, an endpoint:
```py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Generic, TypeVar
from starlite import get
from pydantic import BaseModel

T = TypeVar("T")

@dataclass()
class ResponseWrapper(Generic[T]):
    data: T
    error: bool = False

# something to tell Starlite to wrap some (or all) HTTP handlers responses with a `ResponseWrapper`
...

class Pottery(BaseModel):
    name: str

@get("/pottery")
def retrieve_pottery() -> Pottery:
     return Pottery(name="Teapot")

# Registering the handler in Starlite
...
```

Returns a response like:
```jsonc
// GET /pottery 200 OK
{
    "error": false,  // Metadata
    "data": {"name": "Teapot"}  // Here the `Pottery` resource
}
```

Already tried to use generics in handlers return annotation (used like the pagination feature) but the OpenAPI schema generation broke.
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":13442,"duration_api_ms":14614,"ttft_ms":10481,"ttft_stream_ms":723,"time_to_request_ms":10,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Response Wrapper Pattern in Starlite

Yes, this is possible, but requires careful handling for OpenAPI generation. Here are the best approaches:

## 1. **Middleware Wrapper (Cleanest for OpenAPI)**

Use middleware to automatically wrap responses without changing handler signatures:

```python
from typing import Any
from starlite import AbstractMiddleware, Response
import json

class ResponseWrapperMiddleware(AbstractMiddleware):
    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send) -> None:
        async def send_wrapper(message: dict) -> None:
            if message["type"] == "http.response.body":
                try:
                    body = json.loads(message.get("body", b"{}"))
                    wrapped = {"error": False, "data": body}
                    message["body"] = json.dumps(wrapped).encode()
                except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
                    pass
            await send(message)
        
        await self.app(scope, receive, send_wrapper)

# Register in your app
app = Starlite(
    route_handlers=[...],
    middleware=[ResponseWrapperMiddleware]
)
```

**Caveat**: This wraps the response but OpenAPI still shows the unwrapped schema. Document manually or use approach #2.

## 2. **Explicit Response Model (Best for OpenAPI)**

Return the wrapper directly from handlers with proper typing:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Generic, TypeVar
from starlite import get
from pydantic import BaseModel

T = TypeVar("T")

@dataclass
class ResponseWrapper(Generic[T]):
    data: T
    error: bool = False

class Pottery(BaseModel):
    name: str

@get("/pottery", response_model=ResponseWrapper[Pottery])
def retrieve_pottery() -> ResponseWrapper[Pottery]:
    return ResponseWrapper(data=Pottery(name="Teapot"), error=False)
```

**Issue**: Starlite may struggle with `Generic[T]` in OpenAPI. **Solution**: Create concrete wrapper types instead:

```python
class PotteryResponse(ResponseWrapper[Pottery]):
    pass

@get("/pottery", response_model=PotteryResponse)
def retrieve_pottery() -> PotteryResponse:
    return PotteryResponse(data=Pottery(name="Teapot"))
```

## 3. **Custom Serializer (Most Control)**

Use Starlite's serialization hooks if available in your version to inject wrapper logic during serialization.

---

**Recommendation**: Use **approach #1 (middleware)** for convenience + **manually document the wrapper in OpenAPI** via `operation` decorator if needed, or **approach #2 (concrete types)** for full OpenAPI support.


## 3. **Custom Serializer (Most Control)**

Use Starlite's serialization hooks if available in your version to inject wrapper logic during serialization.

---

**Recommendation**: Use **approach #1 (middleware)** for convenience + **manually document the wrapper in OpenAPI** via `operation` decorator if needed, or **approach #2 (concrete types)** for full OpenAPI support.

What's in this tutorial?
========================

This tutorial builds on the `TODO app tutorial <../todo-app>`_ by adding a database backend using
`SQLAlchemy <https://www.sqlalchemy.org/>`_ via Advanced Alchemy.

We start by comparing a refactored TODO app that leverages SQLAlchemy for data persistence to the TODO app from the
`TODO app tutorial <../todo-app>`_.

We will then gradually improve on the design of our app by utilising more of Litestar's features, such as dependency
injection, and plugins.

Introduction
------------

We start with a full script that shows how you can use SQLAlchemy with Litestar. In this app, we interact with
SQLAlchemy in the manner described by the
`SQLAlchemy documentation <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/asyncio.html#synopsis-orm>`_, and so if you
are looking for more information about any of the SQLAlchemy code, this will be a great place to start.

You'll notice that we use a couple of Litestar features that you may not have encountered yet:

1. Management and injection of :ref:`application state <application-state>`
2. Use of a :ref:`Lifespan context manager <lifespan-context-managers>`

And we will continue to learn about other Litestar features as we work through the tutorial, such as:

1. Dependency injection
2. Plugins

The full app
============

While it may look imposing, this app only has minor behavioral differences to the previous example. It is still an app
that maintains a TODO list, that allows for adding, updating and viewing the collection of TODO items.

Based on the documentation provided, **Tortoise ORM support is not currently available** in Litestar.

According to the changelog [3]:

> The `Piccolo ORM` and `Tortoise ORM` plugins have been removed by this change, but will be re-implemented using the new patterns in a future release leading up to the 2.0 release.

Additionally, **SQLAlchemy is currently the only supported ORM**. As stated in the SQLAlchemy tutorial [2]:

> SQLAlchemy support in Litestar is now provided by `Advanced Alchemy`, a first-party library.

**Current situation:**
- Tortoise ORM plugins were removed and are listed for future re-implementation
- No alternative ORM integrations are documented as currently available
- SQLAlchemy (via Advanced Alchemy) is the sole ORM integration

Unfortunately, based on the available documentation, there is no documented way to use Tortoise ORM with Litestar at this time, and the greenlet dependency issue you've identified with SQLAlchemy on M1 processors remains a blocker if you choose Litestar.

You may want to:
1. Check the [Advanced Alchemy documentation](https://advanced-alchemy.litestar.dev/) to see if there are workarounds for the M1/greenlet issue
2. Monitor the Litestar GitHub repository for updates on Tortoise ORM re-implementation
3. Consider filing an issue if the M1 compatibility is critical for your use case

Based on the documentation, to show exception traces in development, you need to set the **`debug` flag to `True`** when creating your Litestar app [3]:

```python
from litestar import Litestar

app = Litestar(
    debug=True,
    # ... other config
)
```

According to the documentation [3]:
> If `True`, app errors rendered as HTML with a stack trace.

When `debug=True`, the `create_debug_response()` function [1][2] will generate debug responses that display exception tracebacks as HTML (or plain text depending on client capabilities), rather than just logging a 500 error.

Additionally, you can configure exception logging behavior via `LoggingConfig` [5] with options like:
- `log_exceptions: "always"` - Always log exceptions
- `log_exceptions: "debug"` - Log exceptions in debug mode only  
- `log_exceptions: "never"` - Never log exceptions

So ensure your app is instantiated with `debug=True` to see the full HTML exception traces with stack traces in your browser.

ANSWER A:
# Showing Exception Traces

The solution depends on your framework, but here are common approaches:

## Flask (Python)
```python
# Set environment variables
export FLASK_ENV=development
export FLASK_DEBUG=1
```

Or in code:
```python
app = Flask(__name__)
app.config['DEBUG'] = True
app.config['PROPAGATE_EXCEPTIONS'] = True
```

When debug is enabled, Flask's Werkzeug debugger should display full tracebacks in the browser.

## Django (Python)
```python
# settings.py
DEBUG = True
```

Full tracebacks appear in the browser and console.

## General Troubleshooting

If you're still seeing generic 500 errors:

1. **Check if exceptions are being caught** — Look for try/except blocks that might be swallowing errors:
```python
try:
    # risky code
except:
    return "Error"  # ❌ This hides the actual exception
```

2. **Check application logs** — The actual traceback might be in logs:
```bash
tail -f app.log
# or check your framework's log output
```

3. **Add explicit error handling** to see traces:
```python
@app.errorhandler(500)
def handle_error(e):
    return str(e), 500  # Return the actual exception
```

4. **Check the console** — Server output may show the traceback even if the browser doesn't.

**What framework/language are you using?** That will help me give you a more specific answer.

ANSWER B:
Based on the documentation, to show exception traces in development, you need to set the **`debug` flag to `True`** when creating your Litestar app [3]:

```python
from litestar import Litestar

app = Litestar(
    debug=True,
    # ... other config
)
```

According to the documentation [3]:
> If `True`, app errors rendered as HTML with a stack trace.

When `debug=True`, the `create_debug_response()` function [1][2] will generate debug responses that display exception tracebacks as HTML (or plain text depending on client capabilities), rather than just logging a 500 error.

Additionally, you can configure exception logging behavior via `LoggingConfig` [5] with options like:
- `log_exceptions: "always"` - Always log exceptions
- `log_exceptions: "debug"` - Log exceptions in debug mode only  
- `log_exceptions: "never"` - Never log exceptions

So ensure your app is instantiated with `debug=True` to see the full HTML exception traces with stack traces in your browser.

.. tip::
    Just like query parameters, path parameters can convert and validate their values as
    well. This is configured using the ``:type`` colon annotation in the URL pattern.
    For example, ``value:str`` will receive values as a string, while ``value:int`` will
    try to convert it into an integer.

    A full list of supported types can be found here:

The same conversion rules apply to header and cookie parameters: ``FromHeader[int]`` will parse the header value as

Aliasing
--------

By default, the name of the function parameter is used to retrieve the parameter data
from the request. To decouple the two, for example to receive ``camelCase`` query keys
while keeping ``snake_case`` Python parameter names, or to declare an ``X-``-prefixed
header, pass ``name="..."`` to the matching specifier class:

.. literalinclude:: /examples/parameters/query_params_remap.py
    :language: python

[3] (docs/usage/routing/parameters.rst)
.. important::
    Path parameters are always required because they are part of the URL itself. You can
    only get a "missing" path parameter when the same handler is registered against
    :ref:`multiple paths <usage/routing/parameters:path parameters>`
    (e.g. ``["/items", "/items/{id:int}"]``), in which case the handler-side default
    fills in for the path without the slot.

Parameter types
---------------

Path parameters
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Path :term:`parameters <parameter>` are declared as part of the ``path`` component of
the URL using the syntax ``{param_name:param_type}``. The handler function receives the
value via a parameter of the same name, declared with :data:`~.params.FromPath`:

.. literalinclude:: /examples/parameters/path_parameters_1.py
    :language: python
    :caption: Defining a path parameter in a route handler

    parameter. The path parameter is what defines the inout type, and what's reflected
    in the OpenAPI schema. The function parameter is what will be validated against.

Sync and async handlers
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

[5] (docs/tutorials/todo-app/2-interacting-with-the-list.rst)
Path parameters allow you to construct dynamic paths and later refer to the dynamically
captured parts. This may sound complex at first, but it's actually quite simple; you can
think of it as a regular expression that's being used on the requested path.

Path parameters consist of two parts: an expression inside the route's path describing
the slot (e.g. ``{name:str}``), and a matching function parameter on the route handler
annotated with :data:`~.params.FromPath` (e.g. ``name: FromPath[str]``), which will
receive the captured value.

In the above example, a path parameter ``name:str`` is declared, which means that now,
a request to the path ``/john`` can be made, and the ``greeter`` function will be called
as ``greeter(name="john")``, similar to how query parameters are injected.

Question: How To Handle Path Parameters of Multiple Valid Types

We have endpoints where a path parameter can be multiple types (string, integer, boolean).  These are normally used for filtering.  For example:

```
class Widget(Document):
    name: str
    some_bool: bool
    some_str: str
    some_int: int


class WidgetByAttr(str, Enum):
    # Set of widget attributes that we allow filtering on
    some_bool = "some_bool"
    some_str = "some_str"
    some_int = "some_int"


@get(
    path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",
)
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: Union[int, bool, str],
) -> List[Widget]:
    widgets = []
    for widget in await Widget.find(
        getattr(Widget, widget_attr) == attr_value
    ).to_list(length=100):
        widgets.append(widget)

    return widgets
```

The data type specified for `attr_value` in the `path` seems to be overriding the function signature (`Union[...]`).  In other words, `attr_value` is always coerced to a string (never int or bool).

Thoughts?

> The type defined in the path parameter does not have to match the function parameter. The path parameter is what defines the input type, and what's reflected in the OpenAPI schema. The function parameter is what will be validated against. [4]

And:

> Path parameters can convert and validate their values as well. This is configured using the `:type` colon annotation in the URL pattern. For example, `value:str` will receive values as a string, while `value:int` will try to convert it into an integer. [1]

**The limitation:** The path syntax only supports a single type annotation (e.g., `{attr_value:str}`, `{attr_value:int}`), so you cannot declare multiple valid types directly in the URL pattern.

**Solution:** Accept `attr_value` as a string in the path definition, then handle type conversion logic inside your handler:

```python
@get(path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}")
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: str,
) -> List[Widget]:
    # Manually attempt conversion based on the expected attribute type
    if widget_attr == WidgetByAttr.some_int:
        attr_value = int(attr_value)
    elif widget_attr == WidgetByAttr.some_bool:
        attr_value = attr_value.lower() in ("true", "1", "yes")
    # else: keep as str for some_str
    
    widgets = [...]
    return widgets
```

Based on the documentation, this behavior is by design: **the path parameter type (`:str`, `:int`, etc.) in the URL pattern determines how the value is coerced, not the function parameter type annotation.**

From the docs:

> The type defined in the path parameter does not have to match the function parameter. The path parameter is what defines the input type, and what's reflected in the OpenAPI schema. The function parameter is what will be validated against. [4]

And:

> Path parameters can convert and validate their values as well. This is configured using the `:type` colon annotation in the URL pattern. For example, `value:str` will receive values as a string, while `value:int` will try to convert it into an integer. [1]

**The limitation:** The path syntax only supports a single type annotation (e.g., `{attr_value:str}`, `{attr_value:int}`), so you cannot declare multiple valid types directly in the URL pattern.

**Solution:** Accept `attr_value` as a string in the path definition, then handle type conversion logic inside your handler:

```python
@get(path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}")
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: str,
) -> List[Widget]:
    # Manually attempt conversion based on the expected attribute type
    if widget_attr == WidgetByAttr.some_int:
        attr_value = int(attr_value)
    elif widget_attr == WidgetByAttr.some_bool:
        attr_value = attr_value.lower() in ("true", "1", "yes")
    # else: keep as str for some_str
    
    widgets = [...]
    return widgets
```

This approach gives you explicit control over the type coercion based on which attribute is being filtered.

If you need to return other values and would like to extend serialization you can do

You can also set an application media type string with the ``+json`` suffix
defined in `RFC 6839 <https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6839#section-3.1>`_
as the ``media_type`` and it will be recognized and serialized as json.

For example, you can use ``application/vnd.example.resource+json``
and it will work just like json but have the appropriate content-type header
and show up in the generated OpenAPI schema.
